Fnrn PollrrcAl PnncrrcEs CovrulssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620. Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
March 28,2003
Dana Hield Whitson
City Manager
City of Sausalito
420Litho Street
Sausalito, CA94965
Re:
Your Request for Advice
OurFiIe No. 4.-03-007
DearMs. Whitson:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Sausalito City
Councilmember Dennis Scremin regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act').r This letter should not be constnred as advice on any
conduct that may have already taken place. The Fair Political Practices Commission
("Commission') does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice; this advice is
applicable and confers immunity only to the extent that the facts provided to us are
correct and that all of the material facts have bee,n disclosed. (In re Ogtesby (1975) l
FPPC Ops. 71; Govt. Code section 83114.)
QUESTTONS
l. Based on the proximity ofhis principat residence, does Mr. Scremin have a
conflict of interest prohibiting his involvement in decisions of the Sausalito City Council
(*City Council') regarding special use of public recreational facilities located on the
parcel of real property known as the Martin Luther King property (..MLK property')?
2. Based on the proximity of his principal residence, does Mr. Scremin have a
conflict of interest prohibiting his involvement in decisions of the City Council regarding
leasing of facilities or other discretionary land uses on the MLK propi.tyt
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl0l4. Comrnission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
€


File No. A-03-007
Page No. 2
3. Does the "public generally" exception apply with respect to the reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of the above decisions on Mr. Scremin's interest in his
principal residence?
CONCLUSIONS
l. &2. Generally, Mr. Scremin will have a conflict of interest with respect to
City Council decisions concerning the lease of commercial space, discretionary land use,
and special use ofpublic recreational facilities on t}re MLKproperty. When a decision
concerns a clearly defined, specific, and isolated facility that is situated more than 500
feet from the common boundary shared by the MLK property and his condominium
complex, the presumption under the Commission's regulations is that Mr. Scremin will
not have a conflict of interest with respect to that decision.
3. The "public generally" exception does not apply with respect to these
decisions because there is no showing that the financial effect upon Mr. Scremin,s
directly-involved interests in real property will be indistinguishable from the effect upon
a significant segment of the public generally.
FACTS
Mr. Scremin is an elected member of the City Council. His principal residence is
a condominium unit that he owns within "The Terraces,'- a condominiumproject
developed with23 individual, generally adjoining, units, plus outdoorparkingand other
corlmon areas. Mr. Scremin's financial interest in his condominium unit is valued in
excess of $2,000. In addition to this interest, he has a proportional interest in the
cofilmon areas of The Terraces, which is valued at approximately $250,000.
The MLK property is owned by the Sausalito-Marin City School District, which
leases the property to the City of Sausalito ("City') under an agreement that will give the
City title to the MLK property in2013. The MLK property is a former school. The
public recreational facilities on the MLK property are a gyrnnasium, 5 adjoining tennis
courts, a football/soccer field, a baseball field, a dog park and unimproved outdoor
recreational areas. The City has, on occasion, rented the gymnasium and playrng fields to
others for special events. Rental of these facilities is pursuant to the Cityi special events
ordinance, which generally requires that the City Council give its approval prior to such
rentals.
In addition to the recreational facilities, the MLK property includes 6 separate
school buildings that have been renovated and made available by the City for lease by
private businesses. Current tenants are"cottage industries" such as a caterer, artists, web
designers, photographers, and a non-profit day care center. In total, these buildings
provide 55,932 square feet of leasable space. There are currently 28 tenants on the MLK


File No. .4.-03-007
Page No. 3
property and 2vacant spaces that are being advertised for rent. The City Council must
approve all proposed leases of these facilities.
According to data supplied with yourrequest, the common area of The Terraces
shares a common boundary with the MLK property. The distance between Mr.
Scremin's condominium unit and this shared boundary is 69 feet. With the exception of
Building 7 and the open field adjoining BuildingT, all of the facilities on the MLK
property are within 500 feet of the property line shared with The Terraces.
The city's population is approximately 7,400. The city has a total of 4,511
residential parcels divided approximately 50/50 between owner-occupied homes and
residential units occupied by tenants. There are 386 parcels located within 500 feet from
the boundaries of the MLK properly. Of these parcels, approximately 350 are put to
residential use.2
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step
standard analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest
(regulation 18700, subdivisions (b)(1) - (8), which is discussed below. The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest may occur whenever a public official -rk"r u
governmental decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
one or more of his or her financial interests.
l- & 2. Is Mr. Scremin a public official making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?
The conflict-of-interest prohibition applies only to public officials. As an elected
member of the City Council,Idr. Scremin is apublic official. (Section 82}48;regulation
18701(a).) As a City Council member, unless disqualified under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act, Mr. Scremin will make, participate in making, and influence
governmental decisions, including the decisions concerning the MLK property. (Section
87100; regulations 18702.1 - 18702.3.)
3. What are Mr. Screminrs economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from economic interests. These economic interests are described at regulations lg703.l
' Thit infonnation was provided in a March 17,2}O3,telephone conversation with the
Commission's staff.


File No. 4-03-007
Page No. 4
through 18703.5, inclusive. The specific economic interest3 identified by the facts you
supply is Mr. Scremin's interest in real property (e.g., his residence).
A public official has an economic interest in any real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more in fair market value. (Section
87103(b); regulation 18703.2) For purposes of section 87103, "indirect inveshnent or
interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a
public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in
which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 1O-percent interest or greater.,,
You indicate Mr. Scremin's interest in his principal residence has a fair market
value of $2,000 or greater. Thus, Mr. Scremin's residence is an economic interest to him,
within the meaning of the Act. Mr. Scremin's residence is a condominium unit located in
a23trtl;t complex that also contains cornmon areas. Mr. Scremin shares an ownership
interest in the common areas and his pro rata share of the common areas also has a value
of $2,000 or greater. Thus, the common areas of The Terraces are, collectively, an
additional interest in real property that is an economic interest to Mr. ScrEmin. (McGrath
Advice Letter, No. I-02-356; Joehnck Advice Letter, No. A-87-322)
4. WiU this economic interest be directly or indirectly involved in decisions
concerning the MLK property?
A public official's real property is considered to be "directly involved,'in the
decisions listed at subdivisions (a)(2) - (aX6) of regulation 18704.2. The facts you
provide indicate that neither Mr. Scremin's principal residence nor the corlmon areas of
The Terraces are implicated by any decisions the City Council may make with respect to
the MLK property that fall within the above listing.
In addition, pursuant to subdivision 18704.z(a)(l),a public official's interest in
real property is considered directly involved in a decision if any part of the official,s real
property is located within 500 feet of the boundaries of real property that is the subject of
a govemmental decision. If the public official's interest in real property is not directly
involved in the governmental decision, it is deemed to be indirectlyinvolved. .
Inasmuch as the MLK property shares a boundary with the coillmon area of The
Terraces and Mr. Scremin's condominium unit is located 69 feet from that boundary, he
has an interest in real property that is located within 500 feet of real property that is the
subject of a governmental decision. However, in those exceptional ci.crnrrstances when
the govemmental decision only affects a clearly defined, specific and isolated site, such
' In additioo to the economic interests separately listed in section 87103, apublic official always
has an economic interest in his or herpersonal finances, and may have a conflict of interest in any decision
foSseeably resulting in an increase or decrease in the personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities of the
official or his or her immediate family, in the amount of $250 or more over a l2-month period.
(Regulations 18703.5 and I 8705.5.)


File No. A-03-007
Page No. 5
as a specific building on a large tract of land, the Commission's staffhas interpreted this
regulation to allow the distance to be measured from that clearly defined and specifically
affected portion. (Ball AdiceLetter,No. 4-01-279; Krauel Advice Letter, No. l-qZ-
118.)4
For example, under your facts, the decision to lease avacantbuilding, or room
within abuilding, to a commercial tenant is one that affects a clearly defined and specific
site that would not likely have an effect upon the MLK property as a whole. If tht
vacancy is in a building located within 500 feet from the shared boundary of the MLK
property, the decision to lease the vacant space to a commercial tenant would be one in
which Mr. Scremin has a directly-involved economic interest.
Similarly, decisions concerning permits for temporary, special use of the MLK
property's recreational facilities may also be subject to a similar analysis. For example,
an afternoon's use of the footbalUsoccer field for a soccer tournament would affect a
clearly defined, specific and isolated site and not affect the MLK property as a whole.
We conclude that, inasmuch as this field is located more than S00 feet from the boundary
between the MLK property and The Terraces' corlmon area, Mr. Scremin would be
indirectly involved in this decision.
5. & 6. What is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably
foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental decisions on Mr. Scremin's
economic interest in his residential real property will meet this materiality
standard?
Materiality: Not all govemmental decisions by apublic official which impact his
or her economic interests give rise to a conflict of interest. It is when the reasona-bly
foreseeable impact on his or her economic interests is "material" (or important) thai a
conflict may arise. The detennination ofmateriality is necessarily a factual question. In
this regard, we are not finders of fact imd our analysis is dependent upon the facts we are
supplied.
Subdivision 18705.2(a)(l) provides that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on directly involved real property, other than leaseholds, is presumed to be
material. Subdivision 18705.2@) provides that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on indirectly involved real property, other than leaseholds, is presumed not to
be material
These presumptions may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable
that the goveflrmental decision will have the presumed affect on the real property.s Any
a This only works sometimes, because decisions may be interlinked and affect the area as a whole.
This is a highly fact-dependant analysis and must be made on a decision-by-decision basis.
' This is a skict standard. Ifa decision can reasonably be foreseen as having a financial effect as
little as even one penny upon a public official's economic interest in real property, it iri[, in the absence of
an exception, disqualifi the public offrcial from involvement in the decisibn.


File No. ,4.-03-007
Page No. 6
proof relied upon by a public official in this regardmust be reasonable and objective.
(Vadon Advice Letter, No. 4-02-080.) There are specific examples in the Commission,s
regulations of factors thatmay be considered in determining whether a particular decision
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on an official's real property
interests:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;
(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity ofuse, noise levels,
air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood." (Regulation
subdivisions I 8705.2(b)( 1 XA)-(C).)
Foreseeability:Undq regulation 18706, an effect upon economic interests is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. A
financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must
be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I Fppc ops. 198.) In
determining whether a govemmental decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on an economic interest, an official may consider, among otherrelevant
facts, the factors listed in regulation 1S706(b).
Under the unique facts you provide, there may be instances in which the
presumed material financial effect of a decision upon Mr. Scremin's directly involved
interest in his real property can be rebutted. Many of the decisions you describe would
only implement an existing City ordinance. The financial effect, if any, would have been
assessed at the time the special use ordinance was adopted. Decisions implementing that
ordinance might have no incremental financial effect upon fair market value of
surrounding properties.
Moreover, these decisions concern only exceedingly short-term, temporary
special uses of the MLK property's recreational facilities. As a factual matter, it is likely
that these events can be shown, either individually or as a class, as having no financial
effect upon the surrounding properties. For example, Mr. Scremin might determine,
based on clear and objective facts, that a4-hour special event held on aball field, such as
an athletic event or music concert, would have no financial effect at all upon nearby
properties, including his own. This determination must be made on a decision-by-
decision basis.
Nevertheless, given the presumption under our regulations described above,
unless the presumption is rebutted, Mr. Scremin will have a conflict of interest with
regard to City Council decisions affecting the MLK property as a whole. He will also
have a conflict of interest with regard to City Council decisions affecting clearly defined,
specific and isolated recreational or lease facilities on the MLK property, unless those


FileNo. ,4.-03-007
Page No. 7
facilities are located more than 500 feet from the shared boundary between the MLK
property and The Terraces.
7. & 8. The 66Public Generally" and 66LegaIIy Required participation,
Exceptions.
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the "public generally''exception. This exception applies when the
financial effect of a dpcision upon a public official's economic interests is
indistinguishable from the effect of the decision on a significant segment of the public
generally.
Since, under the facts you provide, Mr. Scremin's economic interest is an interest
in real property, the applicable definition6 of a "significant segment" of the public
generally is found at regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(B):
"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the diskict the official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency."
Mr. ScrefiIin's interests in real property are twofold: l) his condominium unit; and
2) his shared ownership interest in the development's common areas. Depending on the
particular decision before the City Council, the owners of the remaining individual
condominium units (all of which share with Mr. Scremin in ownership ofthe common
area) may be potentially affected in a manner similar to the effect upon Mr. Scremin,s
real property interests. However, even if this is the case, collectively these 23
homeowners do not satisfy the 5,000 or 10 percent ownership thresholds above. Thus,
under the facts you supply, the public generally exception does not apply to permit Mr.
scremin's involvement in these decisions concerning the MLK property.
Step eight is an exception that applies when the official is legally required to
participate in the decision. Typically, this exception applies when the participation of a
public official who has a conflict of interest is required in order to convene a quorum of
the public body. You have provided no facts indicating that this exception applies.
6 In your letter, you supply inforrnation indicating the approximate number ofproperties under
separate ownership that are within 2,500 feet from the boundaries of the MLK property. fhis inforrnation
apparently is in reference to former regulation L8707.3, a "public generally" exception that previously
applied in small jurisdictions. Regulation 18707.3 was repealed, effective February 25,2003.


File No. A-03-007
Page No. 8
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (er6)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By
Kenneth
Glick
Counsel, Legal Division
KLGje

that these events can be shown, either individually or as a class, as having no financial
effect upon the surrounding properties. For example, Mr. Scremin might determine,
based on clear and objective facts, that a4-hour special event held on aball field, such as
an athletic event or music concert, would have no financial effect at all upon nearby
properties, including his own. This determination must be made on a decision-by-
decision basis.
Nevertheless, given the presumption under our regulations described above,
unless the presumption is rebutted, Mr. Scremin will have a conflict of interest with
regard to City Council decisions affecting the MLK property as a whole. He will also
have a conflict of interest with regard to City Council decisions affecting clearly defined,
specific and isolated recreational or lease facilities on the MLK property, unless those


FileNo. ,4.-03-007
facilities are located more than 500 feet from the shared boundary between the MLK
property and The Terraces.
7. & 8. The 66Public Generally" and 66LegaIIy Required participation,
Exceptions.
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the "public generally''exception. This exception applies when the
financial effect of a dpcision upon a public official's economic interests is
indistinguishable from the effect of the decision on a significant segment of the public
generally.
Since, under the facts you provide, Mr. Scremin's economic interest is an interest
in real property, the applicable definition6 of a "significant segment" of the public
generally is found at regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(B):
"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the diskict the official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency."
Mr. ScrefiIin's interests in real property are twofold: l) his condominium unit; and
2) his shared ownership interest in the development's common areas. Depending on the
particular decision before the City Council, the owners of the remaining individual
condominium units (all of which share with Mr. Scremin in ownership ofthe common
area) may be potentially affected in a manner similar to the effect upon Mr. Scremin,s
real property interests. However, even if this is the case, collectively these 23
homeowners do not satisfy the 5,000 or 10 percent ownership thresholds above. Thus,
under the facts you supply, the public generally exception does not apply to permit Mr.
scremin's involvement in these decisions concerning the MLK property.
Step eight is an exception that applies when the official is legally required to
participate in the decision. Typically, this exception applies when the participation of a
public official who has a conflict of interest is required in order to convene a quorum of
the public body. You have provided no facts indicating that this exception applies.
6 In your letter, you supply inforrnation indicating the approximate number ofproperties under
separate ownership that are within 2,500 feet from the boundaries of the MLK property. fhis inforrnation
apparently is in reference to former regulation L8707.3, a "public generally" exception that previously
applied in small jurisdictions. Regulation 18707.3 was repealed, effective February 25,2003.

QUESTION: l. Based on the proximity ofhis principat residence, does Mr. Scremin have a
conflict of interest prohibiting his involvement in decisions of the Sausalito City Council
(*City Council') regarding special use of public recreational facilities located on the
parcel of real property known as the Martin Luther King property (..MLK property')?
2. Based on the proximity of his principal residence, does Mr. Scremin have a
conflict of interest prohibiting his involvement in decisions of the City Council regarding
leasing of facilities or other discretionary land uses on the MLK propi.tyt
€
3. Does the "public generally" exception apply with respect to the reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of the above decisions on Mr. Scremin's interest in his
principal residence?

CONCLUSION: l. &2. Generally, Mr. Scremin will have a conflict of interest with respect to
City Council decisions concerning the lease of commercial space, discretionary land use,
and special use ofpublic recreational facilities on t}re MLKproperty. When a decision
concerns a clearly defined, specific, and isolated facility that is situated more than 500
feet from the common boundary shared by the MLK property and his condominium
complex, the presumption under the Commission's regulations is that Mr. Scremin will
not have a conflict of interest with respect to that decision.
3. The "public generally" exception does not apply with respect to these
decisions because there is no showing that the financial effect upon Mr. Scremin,s
directly-involved interests in real property will be indistinguishable from the effect upon
a significant segment of the public generally.

Fax (916) 322-0886 March 28,2003 Dana Hield Whitson City Manager City of Sausalito 420Litho Street Sausalito, CA94965 Re: Your Request for Advice OurFiIe No. 4.-03-007 DearMs. Whitson: This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Sausalito City Councilmember Dennis Scremin regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').r This letter should not be constnred as advice on any conduct that may have already taken place. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("Commission') does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice; this advice is applicable and confers immunity only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct and that all of the material facts have bee,n disclosed. (In re Ogtesby (1975) l FPPC Ops. 71; Govt. Code section 83114.) QUESTTONS l. Based on the proximity ofhis principat residence, does Mr. Scremin have a conflict of interest prohibiting his involvement in decisions of the Sausalito City Council (*City Council') regarding special use of public recreational facilities located on the parcel of real property known as the Martin Luther King property (..MLK property')? 2. Based on the proximity of his principal residence, does Mr. Scremin have a conflict of interest prohibiting his involvement in decisions of the City Council regarding leasing of facilities or other discretionary land uses on the MLK propi.tyt I Government Code sections 81000 - gl0l4. Comrnission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. € File No. A-03-007 Page No. 2 3. Does the "public generally" exception apply with respect to the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the above decisions on Mr. Scremin's interest in his principal residence? CONCLUSIONS l. &2. Generally, Mr. Scremin will have a conflict of interest with respect to City Council decisions concerning the lease of commercial space, discretionary land use, and special use ofpublic recreational facilities on t}re MLKproperty. When a decision concerns a clearly defined, specific, and isolated facility that is situated more than 500 feet from the common boundary shared by the MLK property and his condominium complex, the presumption under the Commission's regulations is that Mr. Scremin will not have a conflict of interest with respect to that decision. 3. The "public generally" exception does not apply with respect to these decisions because there is no showing that the financial effect upon Mr. Scremin,s directly-involved interests in real property will be indistinguishable from the effect upon a significant segment of the public generally. FACTS Mr. Scremin is an elected member of the City Council. His principal residence is a condominium unit that he owns within "The Terraces,'- a condominiumproject developed with23 individual, generally adjoining, units, plus outdoorparkingand other corlmon areas. Mr. Scremin's financial interest in his condominium unit is valued in excess of $2,000. In addition to this interest, he has a proportional interest in the cofilmon areas of The Terraces, which is valued at approximately $250,000.
